coredata.dc:title: The effect of holding conditions in the conventional halide salt process on the performance of Al–Ti–B grain refiner alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Birol, Yücel
coredata.dc:description: Holding the molten alloy at 800°C for 30min under induction stirring in the conventional halide salt method gives the best performance. Higher temperatures, longer holding times and mechanical, instead of induction, stirring during holding lead to an extensive oxidation of the melt and thus to some loss of Ti which in turn hurts the grain refining efficiency. The variations in the performance of grain refiner alloys, however, cannot be accounted for by the Ti levels alone. Mixing of the spent K–Al–F salt is believed to wet the boride particles, impairing their potency as nucleation sites and leading to their agglomeration at the same time. It is thus inferred that even small amount of K–Al–F salt that remains on the molten alloy after decanting poses serious threat to the performance of the grain refiner alloy and is thus claimed to be responsible for the variation in the efficiency of the commercial grain refiner alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metals
Casting
Al–Ti–B master alloy
Grain refinement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The microstructures of the experimental Al–5Ti–1B alloys produced in the present work: (a) alloy 1, (b) alloy 2, (c) alloy 3, (d) alloy 4, (e) alloy 5, (f) alloy 6, (g) alloy 7 and (h) alloy 8.

The grain refining performance test results of the experimental Al–5Ti–1B master alloys produced in the present work: (a) alloy 1, (b) alloy 2, (c) alloy 3, (d) alloy 4, (e) alloy 5, (f) alloy 6, (g) alloy 7 and (h) alloy 8.
The change in inoculated grain size with time after inoculation for different holding temperatures employed in production.
The change in inoculated grain size with time after inoculation for different holding times employed in production.
The change in the Ti content of the alloy with increasing holding time after the salt reaction.
The change in inoculated grain size with time after inoculation for different stirring conditions employed during holding.
The grain refining performance test results of the two experimental Al–5Ti–1B alloys produced with a decanting operation at the: (a) start (alloy 2) and (b) end of holding (alloy 9).
The microstructures of the two experimental Al–5Ti–1B alloys produced with a decanting operation at the: (a) start (alloy 2) and (b) end of holding (alloy 9).
Effect of holding parameters on the Ti recovery in the grain refiner alloys
Effect of decanting practice on the Ti recovery

article.ce:title: The effect of holding conditions in the conventional halide salt process on the performance of Al–Ti–B grain refiner alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Institute, Marmara Research Center, TUBİTAK, Gebze, 41470 Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Holding the molten alloy at 800°C for 30min under induction stirring in the conventional halide salt method gives the best performance. Higher temperatures, longer holding times and mechanical, instead of induction, stirring during holding lead to an extensive oxidation of the melt and thus to some loss of Ti which in turn hurts the grain refining efficiency. The variations in the performance of grain refiner alloys, however, cannot be accounted for by the Ti levels alone. Mixing of the spent K–Al–F salt is believed to wet the boride particles, impairing their potency as nucleation sites and leading to their agglomeration at the same time. It is thus inferred that even small amount of K–Al–F salt that remains on the molten alloy after decanting poses serious threat to the performance of the grain refiner alloy and is thus claimed to be responsible for the variation in the efficiency of the commercial grain refiner alloys.

ce:section.ce:para: The aluminium industry relies largely on grain refiner master alloys from the Al–Ti–B ternary system to control the cast grain size and to improve not only as-cast but also final product properties [1,2]. Today, there is a variety of commercial Al–Ti–B grain refiners which are produced batchwise in induction furnaces by adding a mixture of KBF4 and K2TiF6 salts to molten aluminium above 700°C [3–5]. The halide salts are drawn below the surface of the melt by means of induction stirring, where they are reduced to Ti and B, which then react with liquid aluminium, producing a melt with dispersed particles of Al3Ti and (Al, Ti)B2. Electric power is shut off to allow the spent K–Al–F salt to rise to the surface of the molten alloy where it forms a discrete layer which is removed by decanting. The molten Al–Ti–B alloy thus obtained is transferred to a separate induction furnace where magnetic stirring helps to keep the insoluble TiB2 particles suspended in the melt until casting.

During the investigation of existing grain refiners, two batches of the same product, produced in the same manner with nearly the same bulk chemistry, were found to behave differently as grain refiners [6]. This is not at all unexpected as the microstructure and the performance of a grain refiner are known to be highly sensitive to the processing parameters used in production [6–8]. The conditions under which the molten Al–Ti–B alloy is held until casting are believed to be particularly critical [9]. Recently a revised version of the halide salt method was offered for the production of Al–Ti–B grain refiners with consistently high quality [9]. The spent salt was kept on the surface of the molten alloy until before casting to protect the molten alloy against oxidation for high Ti recoveries. Stirring during holding is thus avoided in this version which relies on a final mechanical stirring action to rejuvenate the settled TiB2 particles. Considering that the electromagnetic stirring employed in the conventional process offers a number of advantages in practice, the present work makes an attempt to find out if the conventional process can be improved further. The potential causes of the variations in the performance of commercial grain refiners were investigated with a particular emphasis on the holding practice in order to identify a set of process parameters that produce the best grain refining efficiency.

Al–5Ti–1B master alloy production campaigns were carried out in the laboratory by the conventional halide salt process on a 1.5kg batch scale. The starting materials were 1070 alloy ingots (99.7% Al) and K2TiF6 and KBF4 of commercial purity. Aluminium metal was placed into a SiC crucible and was brought to 800°C, using a medium-frequency induction furnace. The fluoride salts, weighted and mixed in proportions so as to achieve a Ti to B ratio of 5/1 in the final alloy, were added to the melt at once. Electric power was shut off to allow the spent K–Al–F salts to rise to the surface once the temperature increase in the melt came to a stop. The discrete salt layer was then removed by decanting. The molten alloy thus obtained was held between 750°C and 900°C for 10–120min. This was done in an induction furnace to maintain electromagnetic stirring during holding, customary in industrial practice. In a separate experiment, the spent salt was kept on the molten alloy until before casting to find out if a salt cover could help to improve the Ti yield. Mechanical, instead of induction, stirring was employed during holding in one experiment while no stirring was available in another. The molten alloy in these two experiments had to be transferred to an electric resistance furnace to maintain the intended stirring conditions during holding. The molten alloy was finally cast into a permanent mold. A total of nine experimental alloys were produced by varying the parameters associated with the holding step one at a time, in order to isolate the effect of each parameter on the microstructural features, and on the grain refining efficiency.

The Ti content of the commercial grain refiners is known to fluctuate in a wide range (nearly 20% for Al–5Ti–XB master alloys, between 4.5% Ti and 5.5% Ti). If a particular master alloy has lower Ti than the nominal composition, it is always possible to compensate for the loss of performance by adding into the melt more of the grain refiner. This, however, increases the cost of the grain refining practice at the foundry and is never desired. So, a key quality feature of any grain refiner with an impact on its performance is its Ti content, which, in turn, is dictated by the production parameters. The grain refining performance tests were thus conducted at the same grain refiner addition levels instead of the same Ti levels. Aluminium ingot with a purity of 99.7% Al, weighing 1000g, was inoculated with 4g of each of the experimental alloys. This is the exact amount of an Al–5Ti–1B master alloy needed to bring the Ti concentration of 1kg aluminium melt to 0.02%. The intention in doing so was to elucidate the effect of the production parameters on the Ti recovery, a key quality feature of the grain refiner alloy. If the same Ti level were used in the performance tests by adjusting the grain refiner additions, the very large impact different production parameters have on the grain refining efficiency of the experimental grain refiner alloys through their effect on the Ti recovery would largely be concealed. This performance test is the same as that employed in [9,10] and allows the Ti content of each alloy to impact the grain refining efficiency and to better judge the effect of the production process on the performance.

The inoculated samples were sectioned 20mm from the bottom surface. Standard metallographic procedures were employed to prepare these sections which were etched with Poulton's reagent and then photographed for visual assesment. They were also anodized in Barker's solution and were examined under cross-polarizors for a more accurate estimation of the cast grain size with time after inoculation. The grain size of each sample was measured with the line intercept method at eight different locations in the center and edges and an average grain size was reported.

The microstructural features of alloys 1–7 were quite similar in spite of the variations introduced in the holding temperature, time and stirring conditions in extents far greater than those possibly encountered in industrial practice (Fig. 1 ). Al3Ti particles exhibited predominantly blocky morphologies in this temperature range and were often smaller than 20μm. The TiB2 particles, on the other hand, were invariably smaller than 1μm with a typical boride morphology independent of holding parameters. Minor differences in size and population of Al3Ti particles were reasonably well accounted for by the variations in the Ti content of the alloys, those with higher Ti exhibiting a higher population of and/or coarser Al3Ti particles. Alloy 8, however, had a very low Ti content and thus revealed very few Al3Ti and almost no TiB2 particles (Fig. 1h). This is not surprising since this particular alloy was produced without any stirring during holding. Both the soluble Al3Ti and the insoluble TiB2 particles must have settled due to a lack of stirring and were thus left out of the molten alloy producing after casting, a generally vacant microstructure.

The grain structures of a series of samples inoculated with these experimental alloys are shown in Fig. 2 for contact times of up to 60min. While alloy 8 can, by no means, be rated as a good grain refiner, complete conversion of coarse columnar to fine equiaxed grains occurred within 2min of inoculation with the rest of the alloys. Alloys 1–7 have all produced at least six-fold refinement in grain size, paralleling the performance of grain refiner alloys commercially available in the market. While the visual assesment of the TP test performances of these master alloys suggests a general similarity, grain size measurements in inoculated samples with the line intercept method show that they are indeed different. That these alloys can be ranked in terms of performance implies that the grain refining efficiency of the Al–5Ti–1B master alloys can be optimized by identifying a particular set of holding parameters that gives the best performance.

The effect of stirring practice during holding on the grain refining performance of experimental alloys is shown in Fig. 6 . Induction stirring seems to produce the best results while mechanical stirring falls short of expectations in spite of a substantial refinement it introduces shortly after inoculation. The induction stirring employed during holding is thus claimed to be more efficient with respect to mechanical stirring. The performance of the alloy produced without any stirring (alloy 8), on the other hand, is totally unacceptable with predominantly columnar grains across the entire section of the inoculated samples. The very poor performance of alloy 8 is not at all surprising as both the TiB2 and the Al3Ti particles are largely missing in this particular alloy, produced without stirring during holding.

It is inferred from the very poor grain refining efficiency of alloy 8, clearly linked with the Ti content of this alloy, that the Ti recovery levels are, at least in part, responsible for the variations in the performance of the grain refiner alloys 1–7. While not directly evident from the micrographs in Fig. 1, holding the molten alloy after the salt reaction above 850°C (alloys 3 and 4) has reduced the Ti recovery, by nearly 10% with respect to alloys 1 and 2. Likewise, 120min holding at 800°C has also led to a 10% drop in the Ti recovery in alloy 6. Higher temperatures and longer holding times are believed to lead to a more extensive oxidation of the melt and thus to some loss of Ti which in turn hurts the grain refining efficiency. Mechanical stirring is believed to do the same as it promotes exposure of the molten alloy to atmospheric moisture unlike inductive action which does not create a vortex while thoroughly stirring the melt.
When employing the conventional halide salt method, holding the melt at 800°C for 30min under induction stirring conditions produces the Al–5Ti–1B alloy with the best performance.

Higher temperatures, longer holding times and mechanical, instead of induction, stirring during holding lead to a more extensive oxidation of the melt and thus to some loss of Ti which in turn hurts the grain refining efficiency. Short holding times, on the other hand, leave the salt reaction incomplete and have a similar impact on the Ti level of the final alloy and on its grain refining efficiency.

The variations in the grain refinement performance, however, cannot be accounted for by the Ti level alone. The alloy produced in the manner described above does not refine the grain structure at all when the spent salt is kept on the molten alloy until before casting, in spite of very similar microstructural features and a higher Ti content. The spent salt stirred into the molten alloy during holding in the induction furnace is believed to wet the boride particles, impairing their potency as nucleation sites and leading to their agglomeration at the same time. It is thus inferred that even small amount of K–Al–F salt that remains on the molten alloy after decanting poses serious threat to the performance of the grain refiner alloy and is thus claimed to be responsible for the variation in the efficiency of the commercial grain refiner alloys.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: It is a pleasure to thank O. Çakır and F. Alageyik of Marmara Research Center in the experimental part of this work.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: P. Davies, J.L.F. Kellie, D.P. Patron, J.V. Wood, Metal Matrix Alloys, U.S. Patent No. 6,228,185 (2001).
M.M. Guzowski, G.K. Sigworth, Grain Refiner for Aluminum containing Silicon, U.S. Patent No. 5,180,447 (1993).
